title: End-to-end lineage with DVC and Amazon SageMaker AI MLflow apps
summary: In this post, we show how to combine DVC (Data Version Control), Amazon SageMaker AI, and Amazon SageMaker AI MLflow Apps to build end-to-end ML model lineage. We walk through two deployable patterns — dataset-level lineage and record-level lineage — that you can run in your own AWS account using the companion notebooks.

processed.summary:
- Integrates **DVC (Data Version Control)** with **Amazon SageMaker AI MLflow Apps** to establish a robust metadata bridge between raw data versions and model artifacts.
- Implements two scalable architectural patterns: **dataset-level lineage** for versioning entire datasets and **record-level lineage** for granular data provenance.
- Utilizes **MLflow Tracking** within the SageMaker environment to log DVC data hashes, ensuring that every model run is mapped to an immutable state of the training data.
- Enhances **MLOps reproducibility** by providing a standardized workflow for tracking experimental changes across distributed AWS cloud resources.
key_results: Seamless synchronization between DVC data versioning and SageMaker MLflow experiment tracking.
Implementation of dataset-level lineage using DVC metadata in MLflow run parameters.
Development of record-level lineage for high-precision auditing of individual training samples.
Automated storage of data hashes in S3 to prevent training on 'stale' or untracked data.
Availability of deployable SageMaker notebooks for rapid enterprise adoption of lineage patterns.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Amazon Web Services integrates DVC and SageMaker MLflow to provide standardized end-to-end lineage for dataset and model versioning.

title: Show HN: Mediator.ai – Using Nash bargaining and LLMs to systematize fairness
summary:
Eight years ago, my then-fiancée and I decided to get a prenup, so we hired a local mediator. The meetings were useful, but I felt there was no systematic process to produce a final agreement. So I started to think about this problem, and after a bit of research, I discovered the Nash bargaining solution.

Yet if John Nash had solved negotiation in the 1950s, why did it seem like nobody was using it today? The issue was that Nash's solution required that each party to the negotiation provide a "utility function", which could take a set of deal terms and produce a utility number. But even experts have trouble producing such functions for non-trivial negotiations.

A few years passed and LLMs appeared, and about a year ago I realized that while LLMs aren’t good at directly producing utility estimates, they are good at doing comparisons, and this can be used to estimate utilities of draft agreements.

This is the basis for Mediator.ai, which I soft-launched over the weekend. Be interviewed by an LLM to capture your preferences and then invite the other party or parties to do the same. These preferences are then used as the fitness function for a genetic algorithm to find an agreement all parties are likely to agree to.

An article with more technical detail: https://mediator.ai/blog/ai-negotiation-nash-bargaining/

Comments URL: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47835411

Points: 145

# Comments: 74

processed.summary:
- Utilizes **LLM-based preference elicitation** to overcome the historical difficulty of defining manual utility functions for Nash bargaining solutions.
- Employs a **genetic algorithm** (GA) where the fitness function is derived from LLM-generated utility estimates based on pairwise comparisons of draft terms.
- Bridges the gap between qualitative natural language preferences and quantitative **game theory** models to automate systematic negotiation.
- Features an **LLM agentic workflow** that interviews parties to capture nuanced trade-offs and priorities for multi-party agreements.
key_results: LLMs are used to estimate utilities through comparison tasks rather than direct number generation.
Nash bargaining theory is successfully applied to non-trivial, multi-parameter negotiations.
Genetic algorithms efficiently explore the search space for agreements that satisfy the Nash criteria.
Systematizes 'fairness' in mediation by removing human-facilitator bias in the drafting process.
Automates the transition from raw human preferences to optimized, formal legal/deal frameworks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Mediator.ai leverages LLM-driven preference elicitation and genetic algorithms to automate Nash bargaining solutions for complex human negotiations.

title: Amazon won't release Fire Sticks that support sideloading anymore
